title: Felting a 3D organic shape
subtitle: The core wet-felting technique for building any rounded, hollow form over a resist
excerpt: Learn the core sequence for wet-felting any three-dimensional organic shape: cutting the resist, laying the roving, wetting and rubbing, removing the resist, and fulling to the desired size. This technique is the foundation for bowls, vessels, eggs, and any other hollow form.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felting practice. The resist technique for three-dimensional felted forms is the standard method described in British wet-felting teaching across guild and workshop settings.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method using hot soapy water and agitation to lock wool fibres together into a firm fabric.
A waterproof template placed inside a wet-felt piece to create a hollow form. The felt forms around the resist and is removed once the piece has consolidated.
The stage after initial consolidation where more vigorous manipulation shrinks the felt to the desired finished size.

content.text: form is made: a bowl, a vessel, a slipper, a hat, a hollow egg. The resist template defines the finished shape; the wool is laid around it, felted firm, and then the resist is removed to reveal the hollow interior.
after removal brings the piece to the correct final size. Once you understand the sequence, any organic shape becomes possible by changing the resist template.

items.name: Resist material (thick plastic, foam sheet, or pool noodle section)

content.text: Cut a resist from thick plastic sheeting, craft foam, or similar waterproof material. The resist should be about 30% larger than the desired finished size in each dimension, to allow for shrinkage. For a bowl, cut an oval or circle. Round all edges so there are no sharp corners for the felt to catch on.

Lay roving over the resist on one side, cross-hatching two or three layers. The roving should extend about 2 cm past the resist edge all around. Wet the roving thoroughly with hot soapy water. Fold the overhanging edges over onto the resist back, then cover the back side with two or three more cross-hatched layers.

Rub the outer surface gently at first with a soapy flat palm. After 3 to 4 minutes the roving should hold together enough to turn over. Rub the other side. Continue alternating sides for 10 to 15 minutes total. The felt is ready to roll when it holds its shape when you pick it up.
Step 4: Remove the resist and full

Make a small slit in one edge of the felt and slide the resist out. The felt will be a hollow shell of the resist shape. Close the opening by overlapping the felt edges slightly and rubbing them together with a soapy palm. Full the piece by rolling it under your palm on the mat, squeezing, and massaging from all angles until it has shrunk to the desired finished size. Rinse in progressively cooler water.

items.problem: The felt tears when the resist is removed
items.cause: The resist was removed too early before the felt had consolidated sufficiently
items.fix: Check for consolidation before removing: the felt should hold its shape when lifted and not stretch more than 1 cm in any direction under light pressure.

items.problem: The opening slit stays open and does not felt closed
items.cause: The edges were rubbed together dry rather than with soap
items.fix: Apply a small amount of olive-oil soap to the opening edges and rub them together firmly with the palm of your hand. The wet soapy contact is what bonds the edges.
